data.title: Imperial Petroleum: A Share Issuing Machine, Initiating Strong Sell
data.description:
Imperial Petroleum Inc. (IMPP) is a company that operates a small fleet of tanker and dry bulk ships. Currently, it is trading at a significant discount to its assets.

I believe that this discount is more than justified, following what can only be described as a series of poor managerial decisions.

Misleadingly Cheap

Upon looking at the balance sheet, one might think this is a great investment opportunity. After all, the company has nearly $172 million in cash and short-term investments while owning a modest fleet, which has a net book value of $343.1 million as per the Q3 2025 filing.

On top of that, the company is nearly debt free and is shown as having an attractive P/E and Market Cap as shown by the picture below.

To the un-initiated, this looks like a once in a lifetime opportunity and initially I personally thought the same and wanted to invest in the company. However, upon further investigation, I have found more than enough reason to stay away.

Dilution, Dilution, Dilution

One quick look at the history of this company's shares reveals a different story. One of significant and systemic dilution of shareholders. To put things into perspective, I will grab the outstanding share count from 2022, 2023, 2024 and 2025 (grabbed from the recent equity raise prospectus). I will then showcase the annual growth rate to see how diluted your average shareholder would get over that period. Note that numbers are adjusted for the 1-15 reverse share split that was announced in April 26, 2023.

Year 2022 2023 2024 2025
Shares Outstanding (in millions) 12.97 29.81 34.02 45.75
Annual Growth Rate N/A 130% 14% 34%

Clearly there is a pattern of strong dilution, which would be fine if this was met with significant earnings and asset expansion. I will show a chart of fleet size by dwt to see if the capital raised is being allocated appropriately.

Year 2022 2023 2024 2025
Fleet Size (in thousands of dwt) 808 791 751 1195
Annual Growth Rate N/A -2% -5% 59%

Just to ensure we have a total view of what has happened with dilution and fleet growth, we can take a look at the difference between 2022 and the latest figures. Shares outstanding up 253%, fleet growth up only 48%.

Operational Performance

Naturally, one might say that revenues have actually climbed from 2022, highlighting a counterpoint to my claim that capital was misallocated. Naturally, I will examine this claim and determine if it has a solid ground to stand on.

With $221.5 million spent in capital expenditure, we can grab figures from 2022, 2023 and 2024 to determine if at current, that spending was justified. Note that for 2025, I will be using the Q3 2025 figure and annualizing it.

Year 2022 2023 2024 2025
Revenue 97.0 183.7 147.5 165.6
Annual Change N/A 89% -20% 12%

From what we can see, an expenditure of $221.5 million has actually grown the company's revenues by $68.6 million (or 71%) since 2022, quite impressive.

Yet if we compare this to revenue per share (by grabbing the previous share counts and dividing them by said revenue figures) we see a different story.

Year 2022 2023 2024 2025
Revenue/Share 7.48 6.16 4.34 3.62
Annual Change N/A -18% -30% -17%

As can be seen, dilution overcomes any significant revenue growth with revenue per share dropping consistently year over year. The total revenue per share change has been -52% from 2022 to present.

On top of that, we can see that operational fleetwide utilization has been poor sitting below 85% in 2022 and below 80% in 2023 and 2024. This means that a significant chunk of potential revenues are simply being wasted. There is no excuse for this and despite stronger data in Q3 2025 (88.7% operational utilization), this does not inspire confidence in the operation of the fleet as other peers typically report fleet utilization figures of over 90%.

Related Party Transactions

While the above section alone could simply be written off as unfortunate timing, I believe that this section will show a different story.

Looking at the company's latest 20-F filing, you can see a section entitled "Vessel Acquisitions" which showcases their vessel transactions.

You will note that all of these transactions seem to have a certain caveat to them. That being who the counterparty of the transaction is. In all cases, it is either entities owned by Mr. Harry Vafias, or the Vafias family. This allows Mr. Vafias' private entities such as Brave Maritime Corp. Inc. to be used as a way to effectively transfer operational risk onto shareholders while getting a significant pay out in return.

The fact that the transaction was approved by the company's audit committee does not ease my mind one bit, as it does not change the clear conflicts of interest presented.

In My Opinion, Warrants Prevent Upside

I believe that even if you have reason to believe that the company's management is planning to reward shareholders in the future and that sector fundamentals are positive, you will not get the same return as you could with other companies in the same sector.

Why is that?

This is due to the numerous (over 9.52 million each) Class F and G warrants issued by the company in their recent offering, which place an effective cap on the share price at $6.30 a share (the exercise price of said warrants).

Ignoring the various classes of warrants that are currently way out of the money and likely will never be exercised, there are currently 3.17 million Class E warrants with an exercise price of $2.00 a share, 0.71 million shares issuable at $3.07 a share as part of stock options and 1.65 million shares issuable as part of an acquisition of 3 bulker ships.

The current common share count of 45.75 million excludes these according to the company's statement below.

Considering the above, this represents imminent dilution of 5.53 million shares at current market prices, representing dilution of 12% or a "true" share count of 51.28 million shares.

If market prices were to reach above $6.30 a share, we can assume an additional dilution of around 19.04 million shares effectively capping prices at slightly above $6.30 a share.

Management Can't Change

Clearly a pattern seems to be present with this management team, and you may be asking yourself "Why hasn't management been changed?".

Unfortunately for investors in this company, Mr. Harry Vafias has made sure he has 49.99% voting power through his purchase of 16,000 Series B Preferred Shares. These shares grant Mr. Harry Vafias 25,000 votes per share, granting near total control over the company. The only reason why he does not have majority voting power is because of a clause that ensures that no single person may have more than 49.99% of the total voting power in the company.

Nonetheless, this can be seen as complete power over the company as the other shareholders (of which retail is a large percentage of) cannot meaningfully organize to get a majority of votes against the CEO.

Conclusion and Key Takeaways

Overall, this is a classic example of a value trap. However, I will say that there is one main risk to my thesis.

That being the fact that management may very well prove me wrong and decide to turn over a new leaf and be more shareholder friendly. Such has not happened throughout the history of the company, however, I cannot predict the future or what management may be thinking so this is not a risk I can control.

I set my rating at strong sell and consider the company to be completely uninvestible investment in its current form, and call for investors to stay away from this company.

data.title: Ryder System: Cheap Valuation With A Better Earnings Profile Vs. The Previous Peak
data.description:
Investment action

I give a buy rating for Ryder System (R) because of its private fleet outsourcing growth driver, technology advantage, inflation-protected contracts, and better earnings profile. At the same time, valuation remains cheap.

Company description

R's main business is Fleet Management Solutions [FMS], where it owns and manages fleets end-to-end (from maintenance to compliance, fueling, and ensuring uptime). Essentially, R enables customers to offload operational risk entirely. R also has an asset-light business called Supply Chain Solutions [SCS], where R designs and runs logistics networks. Lastly, R also offers Dedicated Transportation Solutions [DTS] that act as a private fleet for customers (includes equipment, drivers, and routing).

Secular Tailwinds

There is a strong secular tailwind that is driving R’s growth, and that is the sheer scale of the private fleet outsourcing opportunity. To put things in perspective, the US and Canada operate ~4.4 million private trucks. Most of these trucks are owned by companies to meet their own needs (be it selling groceries, making industrial goods, or running retail logistical networks). They are not competent in managing drivers, maintaining the engines, or even navigating the regulatory environment. The problem is as compliance burdens increase and maintenance costs go up, many of these operators are stuck with their private fleets becoming liabilities, not assets. This is where R comes into play. R gives these operators a way to let go of these “liabilities” by converting their in-house fleets into dedicated or leasing contracts. For the customers, this is a clean, lower-risk, and cheaper solution (from a total cost of ownership perspective). Once R controls the fleet, it becomes deeply embedded and very hard to replace.

What really locks this in, though, is the technology, I believe. In the past, logistics was really a black box. Shippers would hand off the freight and basically lose visibility until it arrived at the destination. If a delay happened, they would only find out when it’s too late to salvage the situation. With R, the entire lifecycle becomes transparent through its platforms like RyderShare and RyderGyde. RyderShare gives customers real-time visibility into freight movement across the supply chain. RyderGyde lets fleet managers schedule maintenance, monitor vehicle health, and manage operations from a smartphone (think about the efficiency of this). And we know these tech upgrades are driving conversion because management disclosed that ~1/3 of new dedicated wins are directly driven by them. This shifts the growth narrative to a more structural one because R is not only just competing purely on price (lower total cost of ownership) but also offering a structurally better solution.

Profitability and Continuity

R's revenue model is largely defensive because of its contract structure. ~90% of revenue is contractual, with average terms between three and seven years and built-in inflation pass-through mechanisms. As such, be it wages or maintenance costs that increase, those costs are passed directly to the customer, which is a huge differentiator versus spot-market carriers who basically are at the mercy of daily freight rates. Combined with the structural tailwinds I mentioned, I believe R’s financials are decoupling from the general cycle.

The numbers say it all. It is known that North America is undergoing a freight downturn, but yet R is generating an ROE of ~17%. Compared to the 2018 peak cycle, R only delivered ~13% ROE. Have a think about what that implies. This tells me that R is earning materially higher returns today, in a weak market, than it did in the best market of the prior cycle. Flipping the equation around, if R can earn mid-to-high teens ROE in a downturn, it should do significantly better during an upturn. And my view is the cycle will eventually turn. When it does, we should see strong operating leverage from higher rental rates (the cost of vehicles is fixed anyway) and used vehicle values (this makes depreciation cost per unit lower, and hence, higher margins).

The final piece that gives me confidence R can continue to operate at this level is the planned CEO transition. Robert Sanchez will be moving to Executive Chairman, and John Diez will step in as CEO. For one, this removes a major source of uncertainty. Two, Diez is not a new hire. He was the guy that ran FMS and DTS and served as CFO. In other words, he is literally one of the core guys that helped shape R’s business model today. If I had to pick one CEO for R, Diez is the best guy, in my opinion.

Valuation

Valuation looks cheap to me at 13x forward PE today. You don’t need to make aggressive assumptions to get a nice return from here. Assuming the trough of this cycle is over and R starts to see a very gradual recovery over the next 3 years (revenue growth slowly goes up to mid-single-digit percentage), and net margin recovers to just barely below 2023 levels, R should easily do $675 million in adj. net income. As mentioned, R should have a better ROE than today, and if we benchmark against the previous peak, where R delivered ~13% ROE back in 2018 and traded at ~17x forward PE (late 2017), assuming R to trade at 18x in my model is not extreme.

Risk

The investment case is not without risk. The most immediate risk is a prolonged freight downcycle. If the "lower for longer" environment persists through 2026 or even into 2027, the recovery timeline will be pushed back. There is also the regulatory uncertainty regarding EPA 2027 emissions standards that could delay equipment purchasing cycles, which could hurt demand for R solutions.

Conclusion

I give a buy rating for R because of the structural growth drivers, contract-backed revenue, and strong ROE performance. My core belief is that this freight cycle will eventually turn, and when that happens, R should have a clear path to higher earnings. At today’s valuation, the upside is very attractive even if we assume conservative assumptions.

data.title: Novo Nordisk: Valuation Alone Doesn't Justify Bullishness
data.description:
Generally speaking, I shy away from pharmaceutical companies. But there is a big exception to this. And that would be those involved in the weight loss industry. This is because, in part, I have done a lot of independent research in this space because I ultimately decided, some time ago, to begin taking one of these drugs. And that is none other than Zepbound, which is owned by Eli Lilly and Company (LLY). In the time that I have taken it, I have lost 132 pounds, with 84.4 of those pounds coming off in 2025. So for me, there is a personal angle to this. But up to this point, my focus has primarily been on Eli Lilly and Company.

I figured, coming into 2026, it would be a good idea to spread my wings further. And the next logical candidate to touch on is Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO), the maker of both Ozempic and Wegovy. What I see here is an interesting business. It's actually growing at a nice pace, and generates a lot of profitability and cash flow. Even though its weight loss drugs are expanding rapidly, there are certain competitive pressures that need to be taken into consideration. So even though the stock is cheap on an absolute basis, I think that it makes for a better Hold candidate than it does anything else.

Checking Out Novo Nordisk

Although the pharmaceutical industry has developed a rather poor reputation over the last several decades, a reputation that is not necessarily unearned, there have been positive aspects of it. The fact of the matter is that humans are living longer now than virtually any other time in history. And while there have been multiple contributors to that, such as technological improvements, government spending initiatives, and the like, drugs have certainly been a key component. The management team at Novo Nordisk specifically mentions in their 2024 annual report that the global prevalence of serious chronic diseases remains a problem for millions of people. That year, the company served over 45.2 million people globally, making it a true powerhouse in this space. And because of the drugs that it focuses on, it can do well financially by doing good.

When I say that is a global enterprise, I mean it. The company has research and development facilities in five countries. It has production facilities in 13 countries. And it has 80 countries with affiliates. Primarily, the business focuses on producing drugs centered around addressing diabetes and obesity. The goal here is not just maintenance, but also providing a cure. I already mentioned two of these. They are Ozempic and Wegovy. Ozempic is specifically approved to treat Type 2 diabetes. It does this by lowering blood sugar and is believed to reduce certain risks, like those centered around heart attack, stroke, and more. Meanwhile, Wegovy is approved for chronic weight management and is essentially a higher dose of semaglutide aimed at helping individuals lose weight. There are, of course, others. The oral semaglutide tablet that Novo Nordisk sells is called Rybelsus and its purpose is to treat diabetes as well. But outside of this, the company has other drugs that it provides.

Examples include Resiba, Victoza, Ryzodeg, Xultophy, and Fiasp for diabetes, as well as Saxenda for obesity. But, the company also engages in the rare disease space. For instance, NovoSeven RT is a recombinant factor VIIa that helps to stop bleeding and prevent bleeding during surgeries for individuals that suffer from certain types of hemophilia. NovoEight is a recombinant factor VIII replacement therapy that treats and prevents bleeding episodes for people with hemophilia A. There are other examples as well. For instance, its Rivfloza is an RNA interference therapy that treats Primary Hyperoxaluria Type 1, which is a genetic liver condition that leads to recurrent kidney stones and kidney damage over time. However, it is worth noting that all of these rare disease treatments represent only a small piece of the overall pie for the company.

Over the last few years, Novo Nordisk has achieved remarkable growth. In the chart above, you can see financial performance for the 2022 through 2024 fiscal years, while in the chart below, you can see results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024. It is important to note here that all of these figures are based in U.S. dollars on a constant currency basis. This is necessary because the company actually reports its financial results in Danish krone. Seeing as how I think in dollars, and how I imagine most of my readers doing the same, I did take this liberty when analyzing the company.

With that caveat in mind, you can see how, in the aforementioned charts, financial performance for the business has exploded in recent years. Even in 2025, the picture for the firm was improving rapidly. Revenue of $36.08 billion in the first nine months ended up being 12.3% above the $32.12 billion the company generated the previous year. Net profits, operating cash flows, adjusted operating cash flows, and EBITDA, or increased nicely on a year-over-year basis.

As the chart above illustrates, a lot of the growth that the company saw has come from the success of its Ozempic and Wegovy brands. The latter of these has been particularly amazing. In 2022, it generated sales of only $971 million. But by 2024, that revenue had jumped to $9.13 billion. For the first nine months of 2025, revenue was $8.98 billion. That happened to be 49.3% above the $6.02 billion reported the previous year.

Long term, management believes that there is an opportunity for significant value here. According to the company, there are over 550 million people across the globe who have either Type 1 or Type 2 diabetes. And over 900 million people are living with obesity. Even within these groups, there are smaller subcategories that overlap. This includes 250 million people living with MASH (metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis), which is a progressive liver disease whereby it accumulates in the liver, eventually leading to cirrhosis or liver cancer in some patients. There are over 500 million people living with cardiovascular disease. And there are more than 800 million living with chronic kidney disease. To the extent to which these overlap with obesity and diabetes, the current drugs that the company has can prove helpful. But management continues to make investments in these other markets as well with the hope of expanding its revenue over time.

Normally, you would expect financial performance for the business to justify high trading multiples. After all, as I have detailed in articles about Eli Lilly and Company, that firm is trading at astronomical multiples. But with Novo Nordisk, the picture is different. Based on the data that we have seen already for 2025, for the year as a whole, we should likely expect net profits of about $16.45 billion. Adjusted operating cash flow would be about $22.33 billion, while EBITDA would come out to roughly $24.63 billion.

In the chart above, you can see what this means for the valuation of the company. That chart also shows how the company is valued compared to its results for 2024. Meanwhile, in the chart below, you can see how this stacks up against five other similar firms. On a price to earnings basis, three of the five firms ended up being cheaper than it is. But when it comes to the other approaches, the firm is definitely on the cheaper end of the spectrum. Only two are cheaper when it comes to the EV to EBITDA approach, while it ended up being the cheapest when it came to the price to operating cash flow approach.

Considering the rapid growth that we are seeing in this space, and the success that the company has had already, I would normally assign it a Buy rating. But there are some other issues that I am concerned with. And this primarily centers around competitive pressures. In the image below, you can see the global market share of GLP-1 drugs over time. On the whole, Novo Nordisk has unfortunately seen a decline over the last year.

The only reason why revenue continues to increase is because the market itself is growing rapidly. Make the U.S. branded anti-obesity medication market as an example. As one of the most obese nations on the planet, the U.S. is the place where companies like Novo Nordisk should be fighting for the most. But while overall scripts have skyrocketed over the last couple of years from around 100,000 to around 757,000, Wegovy itself has increased only slightly from around 100,000 to 271,000.

The problem here would be other competitive pressures. Unfortunately for Novo Nordisk, the company has seen Eli Lilly and Company really thrive as of late, with its market share expanding at a rapid pace. In its own investor presentation, Eli Lilly and Company specifically showed how common in the U.S. incretin analogs market, its market share has grown even as Novo Nordisk’s market share has fallen rapidly.

And it's not just the drugs that are out today. It's the drugs that will come out tomorrow. This includes its upcoming triple agonist drug called Retatrutide. On December 11th, of this year, Eli Lilly and Company announced new test results for it. It showed weight loss of up to an average of 28.7%, or about 71.2 pounds in total, all achieved over the span of only 68 weeks. And we also have Orforglipron, which will be its first small-molecule GLP-1 receptor agonist that is on its way to market.

This certainly poses a competitive risk for the company. However, it is worth noting that it continues to work on its own pipeline of drugs. In the image above, you can see its existing pipeline, including its own oral semaglutide. The company is also working on its own triple agonist drug, which could prove a success. But as of now, it's clear to me that Eli Lilly and Company is currently winning this arms race. This doesn't mean that the company doesn't have some things going for it. In December of 2025, the FDA approved the pill form of Wegovy, which the firm launched earlier this month for just $149 per month for the lowest doses and for $299 per month for the highest. Not even Eli Lilly and Company's Orforglipron has received approval as of this writing. This could be a space for the company to compete more effectively.

Takeaway

As much as I would love to be bullish about Novo Nordisk, I can't right now. If I were basing this on valuation alone, I would say that this is a compelling opportunity. But with competitive pressures proving to be an issue, the stock is not cheap enough to justify anything better than a Hold rating at this moment.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: EDIV: High Concentration, High Risk, Poor Long-Term Prospects
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The SPDR® S&P Emerging Markets Dividend ETF (EDIV) aims to provide exposure to high-yielding emerging market stocks. Historically, this is a strategy which has offered relatively poor long-term results. Going forward, given that the fund's yield-weighted approach tends to select stocks in low growth, mature sectors like financials and telecommunications (in many cases with significant state ownership), my future total return expectations for the fund are low.

I am also concerned about high concentration in a small number of countries, with Taiwanese, South African and Malaysian equities accounting for almost half of the fund's assets. Overall, I rate the fund a sell and would suggest that owning a selection of high-yielding single country funds or traditional market-cap weighted emerging market funds may be more attractive.

Historical Performance

Before explaining in more detail why I think EDIV is not a prudent long-term choice going forward, it is worth considering how successful it has been for long-term owners.

As shown below, it has performed poorly since inception with its 51% total return representing ten times less than what you would have achieved through owning the broad U.S. market (proxied by the S&P 500) over the same period.

For those arguing that a comparison with the S&P 500 is too tough, it is also worth highlighting that EDIV has underperformed all of its broad emerging market peers (which lack its dividend-weighted approach). The graph below shows that the low-fee iShares Core MSCI Emerging Markets (IEMG) offered nearly double the total return of EDIV over the past thirteen years (87.9% versus 43.9%).

Concentration Risk

While those buying an "Emerging Markets Dividend" fund might expect it to select high dividend stocks in a way which is proportional to the size of emerging market economies or stock markets, offering diversification across the emerging market equity universe, this is not what EDIV offers.

Instead, it tracks the S&P® Emerging Markets Dividend Opportunities Index, which selects "100 emerging market stocks with [the] highest risk-adjusted yield that have passed stability and dividend growth screens". The other significant rule is that "no single country or GICS sector weight can be greater than 25%, and no stock weight can be greater than 3%".

While this looks superficially fine, it still leaves a lot of room open for high concentration. Technically, the 25% country limit means that the fund would be fine to own equities in as few as four countries, as long as the stocks of those countries were offering the "highest risk-adjusted yields".

It's also important to note that companies being profitable and having three-year dividend stability or growth (the Index criteria) is not a good substitute for quality, as former owners of high dividend Russian stocks like Gazprom or Sberbank will tell you. Indeed, around 5% of EDIV was represented by Russian stocks on March 31st 2021, illustrating the risks associated with buying companies with depressed prices (and inflated yields) due to country risk.

As shown above, EDIV is currently very concentrated in just a few main countries. The top three, Taiwan, South Africa and Malaysia, account for 48.4% of the fund. Meanwhile the top five (adding Thailand and Brazil) represent 70% of the fund, resulting in an excessive degree of country risk, in my view.

The emphasis on Malaysia and Thailand is of particular concern to me, as I have already covered the weaknesses of their respective stock markets in my articles last year on iShares MSCI Thailand (THD) and iShares MSCI Malaysia (EWM). Both countries have stock markets dominated by mature financial, utility or telecom businesses with high degrees of either government or controlling family ownership, giving them less of an emphasis on external shareholder returns and more towards advancing government policy or family interests.

I also think it is worth reflecting on the risk posed by a 21% allocation towards Taiwan. While relations with China seem relatively stable, for now, EDIV's dividend focus could result in further exposure to Taiwan even were tensions to increase. Indeed, price depreciation caused by real geopolitical risks would perhaps increase Taiwan's dividend yields, but this increase would clearly not be a prudent reason to own Taiwanese equities, except from the automatic and passive perspective of EDIV.

Unappealing Yield

Putting concentration to one side, I think it's also important to highlight that, for an emerging market dividend-weighted fund, EDIV's yield is not as impressive as one might expect. It is quite surprising that, given the higher risks associated with owning emerging market equities (and particularly the low growth sectors which dominate EDIV), the yield offered by EDIV is only 4.6%.

While a lot higher than the just over 1% yield from the S&P 500, it's actually lower than the yield offered by some broad country-focused emerging market funds. To offer a few examples, Global X MSCI Colombia (COLO), which I covered in a recent article, offers a 6.9% yield. Meanwhile, iShares MSCI South Africa (EZA) offers 5.8%. I have also covered a number of U.S. stocks recently such as Altria and Western Union, which offer a 7% and 10% yield respectively. The fact that there are high yield opportunities in the United States does undermine the attractiveness of EDIV's strategy, in my view.

Fees

It's also important to highlight the costs associated with owning EDIV. The fund's expense ratio of 0.49% is around the median figure for all ETFs, though its liquidity is slightly lower than average, with a 0.21% bid/ask spread.

While 0.49% in annual fees doesn't seem particularly high, this does result in a 4.8% loss to fees over a 10-year holding period. It's also worth highlighting that versus the broad, low-cost and diversified emerging market alternative (IEMG) shown below, the costs associated with the dividend strategy used by EDIV are high.

Conclusion

I can understand why income investors might want to take advantage of the relatively higher yields available in emerging versus developed markets. However, it is clear to me that EDIV is not a good risk-adjusted solution for achieving these yields. This is because it is heavily concentrated in just a few markets, and within these markets has the tendency to pick low quality names due to its passive dividend-weighted approach. Overall, I rate the fund a sell.

data.title: Goldman Sachs: Macro Benefits Reflected In Valuation
data.description:
Shares of The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS) have been an excellent performer over the past year, gaining nearly 70%, as the Wall Street giant has benefited from volatile markets, significant advisory activity, and elevated debt issuance. These tailwinds showed no signs of slowing down in Q4, as GS handily beat estimates, sending shares up over 3%. While trading conditions may not be as favorable in 2026, it does appear that M&A activity is still gathering momentum. I last covered shares in August 2024, downgrading shares to “Hold,” which was a very premature downgrade, as the stock has gained 90% since then. With updated financials and conditions likely to remain favorable in 2026, now is a good time to revisit Goldman.

In the company’s fourth quarter, Goldman Sachs earned $14.01 per share, which beat estimates by $2.25. Technically, revenue missed expectations by $400 million, but this was due to accounting noise. Goldman is selling its Apple Card portfolio to JPMorgan Chase (JPM) as the bank retreats from its efforts to expand into consumer banking, which never really gained scale. This resulted in a $2.26 billion negative revenue impact, but the net income impact was actually positive as this sale also enabled the release of $2.48 billion in associated reserves. GS generated a 17.1% return on tangible equity. Over the past few years, we have seen GS report solid growth while moving away from capital-intensive principal investments and towards fee revenue. This has reduced regulatory capital needs and improved the consistency of its earnings profile.

Deposits grew by $11 billion sequentially to $501 billion. GS has $238 billion of loans, up $16 billion from Q3. Most of its loans are collateralized and securities-based, and its $20 billion of credit card loans are now being held for sales. Given its focus on collateralized lending and loans to large corporates, credit quality remains strong, with 60bps of charge-offs in 2026. Excluding the Apple Card release, GS set aside $360 million in provisions for credit losses, similar to the $351 million last year and $339 million last quarter. Its charge-off rate should fall substantially in 2025 given the divestiture of its credit card loans. Thanks to loan growth and lower funding costs from Fed rate cuts, net interest income was up 58% to $3.7 billion.

Global Banking & Market revenue was up 22% from last year to $10.4 billion. Investment banking fees jumped 25% to $2.6 billion. Advisory fees were up an excellent 41%, given higher completed M&A volumes. While M&A was quiet in H1 amid trade policy uncertainty, we have seen the pace of deals picking up. I expect this to continue in 2026, given a favorable regulatory environment, especially in the banking sector.

Elsewhere, trading activity also continued to grow, led by a 25% jump in equities to $4.3 billion. Given Q4 2024 was a solid quarter, aided by volatility following the presidential election, this pace of growth is positive. Interest rate and commodity trading were particularly strong. With unclear Fed policy and elevated geopolitical tensions, these sectors should remain firm in 2026. Equities financing activity also hit a record, given booming portfolio lending, up over 40%. This is a high-margin business, and GS’s increased focus is paying off. I expect continued growth here even as intermediation activity may slow, assuming less volatile markets in 2026 than 2025.

In its Asset & Wealth Management unit, Goldman saw revenue decline by 1% to $4.7 billion, though this was up sequentially by 7%. Aided by strong markets and ongoing inflows, this unit has generated sustained growth. With its recurring fee revenue and lower capital requirements, I view the growth in this unit as very encouraging. We are also seeing GS execute on increased cost efficiency, as management raised its target from a mid-twenties pre-tax margin to 30%, more in line with best-in-class peers. The unit now has $3.6 trillion of assets, up $500 billion from last year, and the company has had net inflows for 32 straight quarters, including $66 billion in Q4.

Asset management fees rose 13% while wealth fees rose 8%. Investments were down 36% to $670 million, given smaller gains in private equity. These gains can be lumpy, and with GS reducing its focus on principal investing, this is becoming a smaller part of its earnings. Because of this decline, pre-tax earnings were down 33% to $1.2 billion. I view this level of earnings, with a 15% return on equity, as being more consistent, with normalized results.

GS has been actively increasing its focus on this unit, even as it pivots away from mass consumer banking. Most notably, in September, GS announced plans to build a $1 billion stake in T. Rowe Price (TROW). While Goldman is a leading alternative asset manager with an excellent high-net-worth franchise, this partnership provides scope for GS to leverage TROW’s leading retirement distribution platform and collaborate on products to generate further inflows.

Goldman Sachs has a strong balance sheet with a 14.4% tier 1 common equity (CET1) capital ratio. This is above the 11.4% regulatory minimum. Management likes to keep capital 50-100bps above this level, so it has ~200bps of excess capital, or up to $40 per share. This excess capital can support organic growth, bolt-on M&A, and increased capital returns. Indeed, in 2025, GS repurchased $12 billion of stock. Thanks to buybacks, its share count is down about 5% from last year. Alongside earnings, Goldman raised its dividend by 12.5% to $4.50, and shares have a go-forward dividend yield of 1.9%.

Goldman Sachs is performing well as investment banking activity surges and higher markets lift its fee-based revenue. I expect continued growth in M&A activity in 2026, while current market levels and ongoing inflows support more fee revenue. I do expect some moderation in trading activity (from extremely high levels), as market volatility is unlikely to match that seen in Q2, barring very surprising macro developments. Assuming markets remain near current levels and a ~3%-4% share count reduction, I expect GS to earn $53-$60/share while recognizing that trading revenue can vary meaningfully and in unpredictable ways, depending on market conditions.

This leaves shares at ~17x earnings, or ~16.2x, if we factor in its ~$40 of excess capital. Book value ended the quarter at $357, and shares are 2.7x book value, which is a full multiple for a bank with a mid-teen return on equity. At 17x, GS is trading at a similar multiple to Morgan Stanley (MS), which has a slightly more consistent earnings stream in my view, given its relatively larger asset management mix. That said, if we see a stronger M&A boom this year, that would more notably benefit Goldman’s earnings.

With the potential for meaningful capital returns, given its capital position and macro tailwinds in advisory activity, I would not sell GS here, even as I view its multiple as being full. All else equal, I prefer MS at the same valuation to GS, given its earnings mix, and I rate MS a “Hold” currently, so I do not view GS as a "Buy" after a run of this magnitude. I also view GS as a “Hold” as valuation is not excessive given the macro backdrop. Macro tailwinds support valuation, but investors should expect performance to moderate after an exceptional 2025 for shareholders.

data.title: Chipotle Mexican Grill: Drop Closer To 30, And I'll Think About
data.description:
Introduction

It’s been quite a while since the last time I covered Chipotle Mexican Grill, Inc. (CMG), and since I am looking to diversify my exposure away from the AI growth story of the last couple of years, I wanted to see if it would be a good time to jump in after the company’s share price dropped over 30% in a year.

Back in September of 2023, when I released my first article on CMG, the company’s share price went nowhere essentially, compared to 60% gain of the S&P 500 (SPY). Even with optimistic assumptions, it is still priced at a premium and not a good investment for me.

Briefly on Performance

Looking at the company’s top line performance over the last two years, 2024 was a better year than 2025. In 2024, on average, sales grew 12%-18%, while in the past year, that growth dropped to mid-single digits. 2024 could be seen as tough comps. Nevertheless, the company’s sales are heading in the right direction. The growth can be attributed to a mix of organic growth across all locations, as well as through unit growth, at least in 2024. 2025 was a different story.

If we take a look at the most recent reports, same-store sales began to sharply drop at the beginning of 2025, and continued to decline in the following quarter, down around 4% in Q2 ’25, and in the most recent quarter, it only slightly rebounded by 0.3%. So, the current 2025 growth has been on the back of opening a lot more restaurants across the country, which I’m not sure how sustainable it is when there is no growth across stores.

Let’s take a look at the company’s efficiency and profitability. Margins across the board have stayed more or less the same, with a slight decrease in restaurant-level operating margins by around 100bps to 24.5%. The company is still maintaining the lead in terms of RLOM compared to its peers, like Cava Group’s (CAVA) around 25%, Shake Shack’s (SHAK) 22.8% in the most recent quarter, as well as McDonald’s (MCD) 15%-17%. So, it maintains the lead, but some competitors are getting close. CAVA, for example, at one point had its RLOM reach over 26% in the summer, but then it declined to under 25% the following quarter.

Looking at the company’s utilization of its assets and shareholder capital, we can see quite decent returns over the last two years. ROA is comfortably over the minimum of 5% I require, and ROE is well above 15% I look for; however, this number is definitely inflated due to the company’s use of debt (operating leases). What I particularly like is the company’s return on total capital. According to Seeking Alpha, it is round 15%, which is exceptional. High ROTC tells me that the management has a disciplined approach to capital allocation, with a focus on building out projects that yield higher returns, like Chipotlane, which delivers higher profitability than traditional stores, with elevated Average unit volume, or AUV.

Going over the company’s financial position, we can see that CMG ended the third quarter with around $700m in cash and equivalents, against around $4.7B in operating lease liabilities. The company pays around $500m in operating leases a year, which is covered by its operating profits, as well as over $1B in free cash flow.

Overall, it seems the company has been chugging along just fine. Nothing extremely impressive, nor offensive. Is this good? To me, this is a little boring and concerning because in order to keep growing, the company constantly needs to open new stores, and that is eventually not going to be possible. There’s only so much space a restaurant chain can occupy. A lot of it also depends on the region’s preference and the dominance of another chain there, which may not be possible to expand into. So, it is not a surprise that the company’s shares have greatly underperformed the broad market since the last time I covered it. So, what is going to help the company to regain investor confidence that it is a good investment in the future?

Comments on the Outlook

Recipe for Growth or Disaster?

Over the last couple of years, the management has been highlighting its efforts of improvement across the organization in what could be called a recipe for growth that focuses on traffic weakness, innovation, and capital returns to restore transaction -led momentum that was lost in 2025. The company’s core strategies for 2026 and beyond seem to be focusing on aggressive expansion plans, operational upgrades, and menu innovation.

For the full year 2025, the company is looking to open 315-345 new company-owned restaurants, and 350-370 new restaurants for 2026, with 10-15 restaurants internationally. Over 80% of all new company-owned restaurants will have Chipotlane.

Chipotlane, which is just the name the company gave its drive-thru operations, has been helping the company maintain its RLOM well above 24%. If we look at pre-Chipotlane operations, RLOM was around 18%, so a 600bps improvement is impressive. However, since it was introduced, there has been hardly any change in RLOM, so I’m a bit skeptical that this is going to change in the near future. The management is saying that Chipotlane is vital to reach $4m AUV, as it is a long-term expansion to 7,000 restaurants across North America, as well as acceleration internationally.

In terms of operational upgrades, the company is looking to continue its rollout of the high-efficiency equipment package, or HEAP for short. This is CMG’s kitchen upgrade program, which will feature dual-sided planchas, or a flat-top griddle, so there will be no need to flip the meat, as well as three-pan rice cookers, and higher-capacity fryers, which will save anywhere from 30%-50% on prep time or even up to 70% in some cases. So, even if there is hardly any growth in sales, the new HEAP kitchen upgrades should translate into higher margins across the restaurants. HEAP is the standard in all new restaurants, and retrofitting of existing restaurants began this year, which will take up to three years to complete. Initial results of retrofitting already proved to be an increase in labor/yield gains. Theirs is a lot of work to be done, but so far, Chipotlane and HEAP seem to be very promising, so the Recipe is indeed for growth and not a disaster. It’ll be interesting to see how the new stores perform over the next couple of quarters.

In the short-term however, there are some food inflation concerns that will continue to weigh on the company’s profitability. The management flagged rising food inflation in the latest quarter report, which is accelerating into the “mid-single digit range” due to tariffs and rising beef costs. The management expects this to remain in this range for the whole year and that the costs will not be completely offset, which will put pressure on the margins going forward. This will pass, but it is important to mention since a lot of investing these days is focused on the next few quarters rather than the long term.

Valuation

It’s been quite a while since I did a DCF model for CMG, so I decided to see what a good price could be to pay for it. Last time, the company was wildly overvalued and essentially continues to trade at some ridiculous forward P/E ratios. Currently, the company’s FW P/E ratio is around 35x, which, even after a 30% drop in share price, is still very high. So, I thought I’d use quite optimistic assumptions to see what the investors are valuing this company at. Below are those assumptions.

Analysts see the company’s revenues accelerate over the next half a decade, so I went ahead and modeled the same. It’s not unreasonable to assume this is going to happen because of the management’s aggressive expansion approach. Operating margins, on the other hand, are a little out there in too optimistic territory, but these types of margins are the only way to match what analysts expect the company will earn over the next while. So, the model begins with corporate operating margins and slowly converges to restaurant-level operating margins and beyond, assuming Chipotlane and HEAP are the next miracles in operational efficiency. The rest of the assumptions I felt were good enough, as they were in the past. Below are the EPS projections I get based on the assumptions.

With that said, the company’s optimistic fair value is still around $33 a share, which means the company is still trading above its fair value.

Closing Comments

I really don’t see why investors continue to assign such a premium to Chipotle. I tried to be a lot more optimistic regarding its operations over the next five years, but even then, the company is still not a good investment. The growth narrative has improved since the company’s share price dropped around 30% this past year, but it is still not nearly enough for me to take an interest. If its share price continues to collapse in the future, I would look at it more seriously at around $30 and below, but for now, it’s not the right time for me. My search for diversification from AI plays continues.

data.title: Zscaler: Growth Path Ahead Has Gotten Clearer And Stronger
data.description:
Investment Overview

I wrote about Zscaler, Inc. (ZS) previously with a buy rating, as the new GTM model was working, ZS’s platform is resonating well with customers, and its newer product adoption adds optionality. Fast-forward one year later, ZS performance is much better than I expected. The GTM model is working much better than I thought; it is also securing larger platform deals, with potential for the momentum to continue, and yet it is still trading at a discount to PANW. I stay buy-rated.

The GTM Model Is Working Much Better Than expected

About a year later since my last coverage in early 2025, I think the debate is no longer whether the go-to-market would continue to work, but how material the impact can be (what’s the limit?). I believe ZS has given us sufficient data to have confidence revenue growth can stay >20%. The most important data point we have is that, in Q1 2026, the implied organic net new annual recurring revenue (ARR) growth was ~20+%. You can back into this by deducting the Red Canary contribution from the total net new ARR.

The second important datapoint is that ZS is winning larger, more complex, and high-value deployments. These are not the typical software installations, but an actual commitment to completely rebuild network security (to revolve around ZS). For a company to make such a commitment, they typically don’t churn unless they are forced to (i.e., go bankrupt). But in order for this to happen (ZS to persuade the prospect), ZS needs to have a very thorough understanding of the customer’s environment, which goes back to the new GTM model, that hiring domain experts is the right strategy. There are hard data to back this up, too. ZS now counts ~45% of the Fortune 500 as customers.

Every land (new customer adds) should further lengthen the growth runway, as the new GTM strategy has proven it is able to sell the full platform (includes users, workloads, and IoT, rather than just a point solution). The data that backs this up is the addition of 100 new “Zero Trust Everywhere” customers in a single quarter. Selling a product vs. selling a platform is entirely different because of the ARR velocity, which acts as a multiplier on the deal size. Instead of a linear sales motion where ZS lands small and expands slowly, platform deals compress all this cross-sell potential (spread over multiple years) into day-one revenue, which significantly accelerates the path to >$1 million ARR per customer.

Z-Flex

Z-Flex changes the narrative in the aspect of platform consolidation (I spoke positively about this before), in that I see it as a catalyst to push customers to consolidate their solutions. For context, Z-Flex changes how customers buy ZS’s solutions. Instead of renegotiating contracts every time they want to add users or turn on a new product, customers can scale consumption across the platform whenever they want.

How it works is that a customer will commit to a spending pool upfront (effectively pre-approving their own expansion), which is important because it removes the psychological friction of “approving another spend.” Since they already have a pre-approved budget, when they need a security solution, instead of Googling for the best solution, they would simply tap on ZS capabilities. And they very likely will route as much traffic and consume as many services as possible through that single contract to maximize their ROI. This is why I think Z-Flex bookings have surged >70% sequentially in Q1 2026.

This ultimately creates a powerful flywheel effect that drives up the net retention rate. As customers naturally add in more functionality to fully utilize their committed spend, they become embedded deeper into ZS’s ecosystem, making it harder for competitors to displace it.

AI & Data Security

I no longer view ZDX (Zscaler’s Digital Experience) and Data Security as free options. I now believe they will very likely become key growth drivers for ZS. The numbers make it hard to refute. For instance, the AI security segment grew >80% y/y in Q1 2026. Even if we adjust for the Red Canary acquisition, organic growth across AI Ops and Security for AI is still very strong. Data Security ARR has also already scaled to ~$450 million. For perspective, ~$450 million is close to 16% of Okta’s last twelve months' revenue.

This demand traction implies that ZS is gradually becoming a much more important layer when it comes to enterprise AI usage (data security to prevent sensitive IP from leaking into public LLMs and ZDX to act as an observation layer to make sure these AI workloads can perform). As this happens, ZS becomes increasingly important, and that opens up more room for monetization as the user base expands. Every AI interaction becomes a potential use case, and that means the TAM stops being tied to headcount and starts being tied to usage.

Valuation

ZS trades at 10x forward revenue as of 14th Jan 2026, and I think this is too cheap. This is a business that is compounding revenue at >20% with the potential to accelerate (depending on how fast the platform consolidation impact flows in), and yet it is trading at a discount to Palo Alto Networks (PANW), which has a mid-teens growth outlook (PANW trades at 11x). Just by this comparison, ZS should trade at a higher multiple at least. But being conservative, even if ZS trades at 10x, still a discount, I see a ~50% upside.

Risks

The first risk is execution risk on the Red Canary integration. Any friction in cross-selling could hurt near-term growth, which would weigh on the stock sentiment. The second risk is that with ~45% of the Fortune 500 now captured, the low-hanging fruits are likely gone, and future growth must increasingly come from displacing incumbents in the remaining 55%, which could be much slower and more expensive.

Conclusion

ZS's growth outlook is even better than I expected. The GTM model is scaling, Z-Flex is pushing platform consolidation forward, and AI and data security are growing into key growth drivers. Also, the current valuation seems cheap relative to PANW and its growth profile. I reiterate my buy rating for ZS.

data.title: IYRI: A Solid Fund With Some Likely Limitations
data.description:
Investors who are seeking income have a lot of options in the market today. While traditional dividend investors focused on income, choosing from a variety of fairly well-known companies and some mostly large-cap funds, today the investing landscape has far more choices for these individuals.

Two of the more appealing options for income investors have been REITs and covered-call funds. A new exchange-traded fund that came out in January 2025, the NEOs Real Estate High Income ETF (IYRI), offers investors a real estate based investing option that also uses an options strategy.

IYRI has offered investors total returns of 9.43% since the fund's inception in early 2025, while the S&P 500 (SPY) has offered investors total returns of 18.41% during the same time period, and the Dow Jones US Real Estate Index that this ETF tracks, has offered investors total returns of 6.81% during the last 12 months.

Today, I am initiating coverage of the NEOs Real Estate High Income ETF with a buy rating. This ETF tracks the Dow Jones U.S. Real Estate Capped Index (DJUSRE) and also uses a similar options strategy that the successful NEOs funds have used, such as NEOs S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI) and the NEOs NASDAQ-100 High Income ETF (QQQI). The outlook for REITs also remains positive, with a consensus being for rates expected to slowly move lower and these assets to generally benefit from the heavy power usage the move to AI is supposed to require.

The NEOs Real Estate High Income ETF has an expense ratio of 0.68%, a distribution rate of 10.83%, and $186.14 million in assets under management. The fund tracks the Dow Jones U.S. Real Estate Capped Index and is invested 98.88% in real estate. IYRI's four largest holdings are Welltower (WELL), Prologis (PLD), American Tower Corporation (AMT), and Equinix (EQIX). These positions make up nearly 28% of the overall ETF's real estate assets.

The similar options strategy that IYRI uses, which is common to most of the NEOs funds, is that this ETF both buys and sells call options at the discretion of the managers. IYRI generally buys and sells index call options that are 1-6%t out-of-the-money, going out 10-30 days. The fund uses a data-based approach and modifies strategies for varying market conditions. IYRI seeks to provide tax-efficient income, and the fund's payouts are often characterized as return of capital.

The NEOs Real Estate High Income ETF paid out $5.69 of income for a yield of 11.56% over the last year. The investment's distributions have also been in a very tight range during the 12-month period, varying from $0.44 a share to $0.51 a share, with payouts being higher more recently as volatility levels have been rising.

The outlook for data centers remains favorable, with this sector expected to benefit over the next decade from what is forecasted to be the massive move to AI during that period, since this new form of technology uses a significant amount of power.

Valuation levels for the core holdings of IYRI appear reasonable as well. Welltower's debt-to-equity ratio of 46% remains well below the industry average of nearly 96%. Prologis's debt-to-equity ratio also remains well below the industry average at 62.79%. American Tower's net debt to EBITDA ratio of 4.96 is also more favorable than most competitors.

All investment theses have risks, and the NEOs Real Estate High Income ETF faces some standard potential challenges. While rates have been moving down, if inflation were to accelerate, then the Fed would obviously like to become more hawkish, negatively impacting REITs. IYRI also has some exposure to more struggling commercial real estate assets, such as the Simon Property Group (SPG), although recent economic data shows consumer spending is holding up. This ETF also only has one year of data available.

IYRI has done an excellent job of tracking the underlying Dow Jones U.S. Real Estate Capped Index it tracks, and the fund has also paid out consistent and substantive income over the last year as well. While growth remains slow, the economy continues to show resiliency, and the most likely scenario is for the market to continue to slowly rise, with earnings expectations generally still strong for 2026. Indeed, while REITS are likely to be growth investments, tech-based data centers should continue to do well, and this sector should be able to offer investors solid returns.

data.title: Cohen & Steers: Focus On Latest Products And New Appointments (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
My rating for Cohen & Steers, Inc.'s (CNS) is revised from Hold to Buy.

I've turned positive on the name, after evaluating its key corporate developments.

My July 29, 2025 article touched on its worse-than-expected 2Q25 numbers and a potential return to inflows in the medium-term.

Product Introductions Draw Attention

Last month, CNS revealed the expansion of "its suite of actively managed exchange-traded funds (ETFs) with the launch of two new strategies." These are "the Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Opportunities Active ETF (CSIO)" and "the Cohen & Steers Short Duration Preferred and Income Active ETF (CSSD).

CSIO/CCSD At A Glance

My take is that this is good for CNS in many ways.

The firm had come up with its maiden three ETFs in 1Q25. They were "Cohen & Steers Preferred and Income Opportunities Active ETF (CSPF)," Cohen & Steers Real Estate Active ETF (CSRE)," and "Cohen & Steers Natural Resources Active ETF (CSNR)."

CSNR, CSRE, and CSPF outdid their benchmarks by +4.9ppts (percentage points), +2.2ppts, and +1.2ppts, respectively on a "Life-to-Date/LTD" basis as of Sep 30, 2025. I obtained these figures from CNS's Q3 briefing in mid-October last year.

It's reasonable to think that flows will accelerate for the company's ETF-focused offerings, considering the above-mentioned alpha generation. CNS is wise to build on the positive momentum by rolling out CSSD/CSIO.

I'm expecting robust demand for CSIO.

SA analyst "Tangerine Tan Capital" has predicted "an ETF focused on AI infrastructure will most likely outperform the broader market" this year. He or she believes that forward-looking AI-related "capital expenditures are underestimated."

CNS also shared its view at the 3rd quarter call that "allocations" to "real asset strategies that are inflation sensitive probably should be going up." Its internal analysis indicates that "elevated inflation is likely to be a multiyear" phenomenon.

Looking ahead, the group's FY26 profitability prospects are promising.

Most of the non-variable launch-related expenses for CSNR/CSPF/CSRE/CSIO/CSSD should have been incurred in the prior year. That translates into favorable operating leverage effects, which bodes well for its future margins.

New Hires Signal Ambitions

Earlier in the week, the company shared that "Diana Shieh" will be the "Chief Operating Officer (COO) and Head of Asset Management" for Cohen & Steers' Private Real Estate Group. According to the disclosure, she boasts "over 20 years of real estate investment and operational experience" and previously managed a "$20 billion US portfolio" for "Oxford Properties Group."

CNS has been doing well in the unlisted properties space.

According to its Q3 results meeting, the firm secured $0.24B worth of capital commitments for its "first closed-end drawdown fund, Cohen & Steers Real Estate Opportunities Fund" last year. At the briefing, CNS also noted that the "nontraded REIT Cohen & Steers Income Opportunities REIT has continued its industry-leading investment performance" in '25 year-to-date.

Getting in a heavyweight like Shieh will ensure that "Private Real Estate" will become a major growth engine for CNS in the future.

Separately, "Kikuo Shirose", the former "President & Chief Executive Officer of PineBridge Investments Japan", recently "joined Cohen & Steers as Representative Director of Cohen & Steers Japan Limited." This information was taken from a January 7 announcement.

The firm is too concentrated in its home market now, with "North America" representing over three-quarters of its AUM. It's sensible to make a push into non-U.S. markets like Asia.

I wrote in the late-Jul '25 update that CNS "established a new office in Hong Kong" in Q2 2025 to "attract Asian investors." The recruitment of "Kikuo Shirose" is another step in the right direction.

Anticipating A Fourth Quarter Beat

CNS's Q4 2025 results will be published next Thursday on Jan 22.

It released the "Preliminary Assets Under Management and Net Flows for December 2025" on Monday.

Its net inflows improved from Q3 2025's +$0.23B to Q4 '25's +$1.2B.

Also, the group saw "Assets Under Management/AUM" rise +12% year-on-year to $90.5B as of end-2025.

But the sell-side is projecting a YoY top line deceleration from +6.4% in Q3 '25 to +3.2% for Q4 '25 as per S&P Capital IQ. The forecasted 4th quarter bottom-line growth of +4.5% is also inferior to Q3's +5.2%.

There's a disconnect between the consensus estimates and CNS's actual operational performance. Its ETF additions and leadership changes also make me confident about its outlook. My bet is on an above-consensus Q4 showing.

You Shouldn't Ignore These Variant Views

It's possible that the latest recruitments don't work out, because of conflicts between existing staff and the newcomers.

CNS's ETFs could face tough competition from similar products launched by its rivals.

My Closing Thoughts

I'm a bull on CNS now.

I find the firm's recent product- and management-related initiatives encouraging.

Its forward FY26 P/E is 20.5 times (source: S&P Capital IQ).

CNS's capital appreciation potential is +22% (25/20.5) benchmarked against its own 3-year mean earnings multiple of 25x.

data.title: Why Investors Should Look Past Geopolitics And Focus On Fundamentals
data.description:
There’s been no shortage of headlines vying for investors’ attention. Damian Fernandes, Co-Head of Global Equity Portfolio Management with TD Asset Management, says beyond the noise, economic fundamentals remain strong, which will likely bode well for markets.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: There are no shortage of headlines out there grabbing our attention as investors and only a couple weeks into the year. Our feature guest today says, if you can get past all that noise, fundamentals actually look pretty good. Joining us now with more is Damian Fernandes, co-head of Global Equity Portfolio Management with TD Asset Management.

Damian, great to have you. I like having you here when there's too many headlines hurting my head because I know you can cut through them.

Damian Fernandes: It's always a pleasure, Greg. And can we still say happy New Year? Or is it just the influx of headlines is just overwhelming it? Like, we're debating if it's going to be happy.

Greg Bonnell: It's felt like a year already. Is 2026 over yet?

Damian Fernandes: Exactly. We've gone right into it. And in your intro, you had a few ideas that you were talking about, the headlines coupled with the weakness and stuff, and I think people still haven't looked at the big picture.

And the big picture is like, yes, the president of the United States has had an active first two weeks, right? If we just list between both geopolitical and domestic agendas, we had the audacious extraction of Maduro in Venezuela.

Greg Bonnell: That feels like months ago.

Damian Fernandes: That feels like months ago, right? Because it's just the shelf life of these news-- because it happens so quickly. It feels like it's collapsing. But then we also had, maybe, defense stocks will no longer be able to pay dividends. And that was followed with, hey, let's try to address affordability.

And maybe we'll have the GSEs, the Fannies and Freddies, buy mortgages. That was followed with credit card-- you were talking about this in the introductory comments. The banks are facing pressure now because he wants to cap credit card rates, and yesterday's interchange fees. And then it was talking about limiting for these hyperscalers in terms of reducing costs for utility bills.

And I'm sitting there, and it's like, today's the 14th of Jan. This is all-- [LAUGHS]. This has happened the last two weeks. And I can see how your investors are looking at this and are already feeling a sense of nervousness, agitation, concern, because it's happening so quickly. But the big picture, which is to your point, it's that we're in an economic recovery.

In fact, when you look at the breadth of the market, it's the median stocks actually moving higher. Inflation was reported yesterday. Inflation is headed in the right direction. That gives the Fed ammunition to-- well, it's not the next meeting they're going to cut rates, but we're looking to rate cuts as we progress through the year. So we have economic growth, rate cuts, and we still have transformational improvement helping productivity from AI.

And so I guess, taking a step back, we had a positive year last year. We're starting the year with a fair amount of noise. But the big picture, the fundamentals, still remain constructive. And that's where we sit.

Greg Bonnell: From day to day, markets will move. As we said, the S&P 500 is down about a percent today, the tech-heavy NASDAQ a little bit more than that. I think people-- at some point, too, when you have a run, a bull run, that has been this strong, they don't like the down days. And you're saying, don't be surprised, though, if we get more downside. Would that still be constructive as a part of a bull market?

Damian Fernandes: Sure. Look, we've just come from a banner year in global markets last year, right? I talked about this the last time. I think Donald Trump, perversely, in making America great again, has made global equities great again. The TSX--

Greg Bonnell: We outperformed.

Damian Fernandes: The TSX was one of the best-performing markets. Europe had-- some markets were high 20s. Japan, Hong Kong were high 20s, 30s. What's actually happened is Donald Trump's antagonism on certain policies-- whether it be trade or defense--- have forced a lot of global markets and these countries to take measures to improve their domestic growth.

In Canada itself, we were talking about last year about what really matters, about promoting growth-enhancing initiatives, whether it be trying to reduce cross-border dislocations in terms of just cross-border improvements, in terms of infrastructure. So I do think it's actually-- the move towards what the US has done in becoming a little more insular has actually created incentives for a lot of countries to improve their own domestic trend growth. That's really positive.

And we've had a really strong year, right? Mark to market last year, the US was up double digits, Canada north of 20. And I think this year, when investors are coming in-- people like calendarizing years. It's like, OK. It's like, start from zero.

Greg Bonnell: Everything changes. January 1, we're all new people.

Damian Fernandes: Yeah, exactly. Just totally like-- give it a month and the resolutions will go out the window. But I think what people have to forget is that-- look, we should be defensive and we should be cautious when you have macro variables not in your favor. For example, when central banks are on a tightening campaign, when inflation is increasing, when growth is slowing, when earnings revisions are falling.

I've named you all-- those are negative measures where I would be cautious. But mark to market--

Greg Bonnell: Those things aren't happening.

Damian Fernandes: --those things aren't happening. So, look, could we see it pull back? Of course we could. Would I ever come here and say, I know with certain degree, if-- I don't have a crystal ball. 10% pullbacks, even in very healthy years-- think about 2021 or 2018. They do happen, and they happen just because the market needs to-- think of a pressure cooker. You need to release some steam. There's a lot of excitement being built up, and any small dislocation, a Trump tweet, can get people nervous and they'll sell. But those should be viewed as buying opportunities.

Greg Bonnell: Now, you were talking about the fact that we are seeing better breadth in the markets, and we have been worried about concentration, namely in the Magnificent 7. I'm not going to steal your line, because you said it to me yesterday, the Mag 7 is now the--

Damian Fernandes: The Lag 7, right?

[LAUGHTER]

Well, it's not longer-- there's no unanimity. Before, all of those Mag 7 stocks were appreciating by a certain degree. Outside of Google, and to a certain degree, maybe, depending on when you start on Tesla, most of the other Mag 7s actually lagged. Microsoft (MSFT) and Meta (META) and Amazon (AMZN), since July last year--

Greg Bonnell: I think we can throw up a chart with a lot of lines on it, but we got the S&P 500 and a lot of these Mag 7 stocks in comparison. You can start seeing what the last six months look like.

Damian Fernandes: Yeah, look at that. There's negatives. And contrast that-- so Microsoft is off 8%, Meta's off 12%, and Amazon's up 6%. But that's kind of lagged the market, right? Because from July last year, the market's up double digits.

So what's actually happening is-- I don't want to say-- like, these companies are awesome businesses. They're quasi-monopolists in their things. They have immense pricing power. But what's actually happened is that the generals are taking a rest. These are the generals that actually led this bull market from the bull market recovery in 2022. They were-- and enabled by the AI spend and AI capabilities.

But they're actually taking a breather right now. And to a certain degree, the fundamentals on them, while great, they are decelerating. Now, think about the rate of change, the second derivative. Earnings growth is still going to be positive. Revenues are still going to grow. But the rate of growth is going to slow.

And to your point about-- for the last few years, we've had bad breadth. I mean the market's had bad breadth. And I mean that in, it was these Mag 7 stocks that were actually pulling the market higher. Since the middle of last year, especially since Jackson Hole and the Fed, particularly, committing to a path of lower rates, you've seen the median stock outperform. You've seen places like consumer being some of the strongest sectors--

Greg Bonnell: I think on that chart, too, we had the Russell.

Damian Fernandes: Yeah, the Russell 2000. Small caps are moving. And the S&P-- if you look at what's happened, 2021 was a really strong year. 2022 was a bear market year, we were off almost 20%. The S&P broke above the 2021 highs all the way back in 2023-2024. The Russell just broke its previous highs from 2021 a few months ago.

So, there's a lot of catch-up in small-caps. There's a lot of catch-up in the median stock. And these stocks are the ones that-- like, Microsoft doesn't benefit from lower rates. Microsoft is debt-free. It's got a better credit rating than the US government. It doesn't care what the Federal Reserve is doing cutting rates.

But your consumer stock that has a fair amount of leaseholds or debt, it actually sees a much bigger rate of change in its profitability if growth is fine and if rates are being lowered. So what's actually happening is-- and what normally happens in markets-- is that when people are concerned about growth, they will bid up these quality stocks which have bulletproof business models where there's certainty.

But when people are comfortable about economic growth, people will move away from the certainty towards where the growth is going. And the growth is going towards the smaller-cap stocks, the stocks in the cyclical sectors. That's where we're seeing a lot of positive earnings revisions and momentum in stock prices.
